segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They found Thornik behind the smithy, where the local smith had let him use the corner of a workbench.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd laid his tools out in a row -- not randomly, but the way a soldier lays out equipment for inspection, each item in its place, each absence accounted for. The empty charge-loops from his harness. A cracked lens.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The housing for a device that no longer had a mechanism to house. The things that had survived the mountain and the siege and could be carried forward. The things that couldn't.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Between them, like a border marker between countries, lay the dragon-dwarf chisel -- the one from his grandfather's maps, the etchings worn to suggestion, the kind of thing you had to know was there to see.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I'm leaving,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said, without looking up. The words came out heavier than he'd perhaps intended, landing on the stone floor of the smithy like something dropped.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Not because I can't scrape up gear in Millbrook. I could. There's a metalworker on the east lane who does passable work, and I've made do with less."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He picked up the chisel and turned it in his hands.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The firelight caught the faint etchings -- dwarven script so old it predated the current alphabet, letters that curved where modern runes angled, from an age when the clans still wrote in circles because straight lines were considered presumptuous.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I'm leaving because I have to stand in front of my clan and say my grandfather wasn't mad. He was right."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He set the chisel down and began wrapping it in oilcloth, slowly, with the care of someone performing a small ceremony -- the kind you invent because the real ceremonies were lost with the people who used to perform them.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "His journals, his maps, the chisel -- if I don't put them in Deepstone hands myself, he stays a punchline and my name goes with him. That matters where I come from. It matters to me."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin watched him wrap the chisel. He thought about the mountain, about the vault that had opened for Thornik's key and no one else's. About a dead man's journal written in blood and a living grandson who'd carried it through fire.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He thought about what it costs to be right for thirty years in a room full of people who think you're wrong.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I'll retool at home,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik continued.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Proper charges, lenses that don't crack when you look at them sideways, a housing that doesn't rattle like a drunk uncle at a wedding."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He almost smiled.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "But that's not the reason. The reason is lineage. And truth. And a stubborn old dwarf who deserved better than a footnote."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He turned to each of them, and this part he'd clearly thought about -- each gift chosen with the careful precision of a man who'd spent his life measuring things and knew exactly what each person needed.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: To Vex, he held out a slim leather case, small enough to disappear into a palm. She opened it: three smoke-sticks, the kind that went off on impact and made it hard to see anything for thirty seconds.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Disappearing, not winning,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Don't be clever with them. Just go."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "You've met me,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "That's why I only gave you three."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He held her gaze.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "One for each time you should have run and didn't."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She tucked them away without looking at them, which was how Vex showed she was moved.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: To Caelin, an iron ring the width of a thumb, one quadrant filed to a gap. It sat heavy in the palm -- heavier than iron had a right to be, warm from Thornik's hands.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "At dusk on Sixthday, tap three-one-two on any stone. If I'm above ground and within a week's march, it'll hum toward me."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He pressed it into Caelin's palm.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Don't lose it. I only made two and the other one's mine."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin turned the ring. The gap caught the light.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "How does it work?"

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Resonance. The same principle as the chasm bridge -- sympathetic vibration across a tuned frequency. Mine and yours ring at the same pitch. Doesn't matter how much stone is between them."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He shrugged, as if the engineering of a device that could locate a specific person across a mountain range was roughly as complicated as building a birdhouse.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Dwarf work. We're good at making things that find each other."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: To Serana, last, a gear-coin stamped with the Deepstone mark -- a small bronze disc the size of a knuckle, the clan symbol cut so fine it caught fingerprints like a mirror.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "If I go quiet too long, show this at any Deepstone hall. They'll know how to find me."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He held her gaze.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I'm not planning to go quiet."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "I know,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said, and took the coin with both hands -- the way you take something from someone when you want them to feel you taking it.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I hate leaving you,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he finished, and his voice went rough at the edges in the way of people who don't usually let it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The gruff warmth that had carried them through cold camps and bad tunnels and worse food -- the voice that had told stories about cousins with independent eyebrows and made tea from minerals that should not have been drinkable -- cracked, just once, and then steadied.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "But if I stay, I'm another body with the wrong tools. If I go, I come back with the right kit and proof that my grandfather's name means something. And he stops laughing alone."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex tucked the smoke-case into her jacket.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Try not to die of politics."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "No promises,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said, and almost smiled.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "You know how clans are."
